title: Cat Squatting to Pee But Only Drops Come Out: Urgent Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is straining to urinate, it may be a life-threatening blockage. Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and what you should do.
content_html:
Understanding Urinary Straining in Cats

When a cat repeatedly squats to urinate but only produces tiny drops or no urine at all, it is usually a sign of a serious condition known as Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a urethral obstruction. This happens when the urethra is partially or fully blocked by crystals, stones, or inflammatory debris, preventing the bladder from emptying.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. This is considered a high-priority medical emergency. A urinary blockage is life-threatening because it causes toxins to build up in the bloodstream and can lead to kidney failure or a ruptured bladder in as little as 24 hours. If your cat is also vocalizing in pain, licking their genitals excessively, or acting lethargic, you must seek immediate emergency veterinary care. This is especially critical for male cats, whose anatomy makes them highly susceptible to total blockages.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If your cat has managed to pass any fluid, taking a quick photo of the litter box or the area where they attempted to go can be very helpful for the veterinary team. A photo helps the vet see the color of the urine—such as whether it contains blood—and the exact volume of output. Providing this visual information during triage helps the medical team quickly assess the severity of the obstruction and prioritize your cat's treatment upon arrival.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if my cat gets better?
faqs.answer: No. If your cat is unable to pass urine, their condition can deteriorate rapidly. Delaying treatment can lead to heart rhythm issues and organ failure. It is always better to be safe and have them evaluated immediately.

faqs.question: Is this common in female cats too?
faqs.answer: While female cats can suffer from urinary tract infections and inflammation (cystitis) that cause straining, a total physical blockage is much more common in male cats due to their narrower urethra. However, any cat unable to urinate needs emergency care.

faqs.question: What are the other signs of a urinary blockage?
faqs.answer: Common signs include frequent trips to the litter box, crying out while squatting, blood in the urine, loss of appetite, and vomiting. If your cat's abdomen feels hard or painful to the touch, seek help immediately.
